data.text:
I've got a solid grasp of the situation now. The plan is to set up a worktree with a new branch, then create a subcrate within it to redevelop the containers library in Verus with the goal of proving the semi-persistent vector implementation.

Looking at the existing setup, I can see the abstract-domains crate uses Verus version `0.2026.04.12.f1166c4` with its own `rust-toolchain.toml` and is built separately via `cargo verus verify`. The semi-persistent Vec has a specific API—`push`, `pop`, `get`, `set`, `len`, `mark`, `restore`—backed by state including a store, diff log, frames, and fork tracking. The core correctness properties I need to establish are functional equivalence with a spec-level sequence and the O(1) mark/O(k) restore performance guarantees.

My verification strategy will phase this carefully: start with `ParallelStore<T, u32, true>` since it avoids bit-stealing complexity, then move to the inline store variant, token validation, and finally other containers. I'll follow the existing naming convention with `semi-persistent-containers-verus` for both the crate and worktree directory.

I should also account for Verus's toolchain quirks—it uses its own nightly build—and the fact that const generics support is still evolving, so I might need to start with monomorphic types and generalize later. The spec view will be straightforward (just `Seq<T>` for the data), but modeling the tag stripping in InlineStore and the ForkHistory token logic will require careful abstraction.

For the proof structure, I'm defining spec functions for the current view and the state at each frame, then establishing an invariant that replaying the diff log from any frame's checkpoint recovers that frame's state. This captures the semi-persistent property: after a mark, the top frame matches the current state, and after a restore, the current state equals what was saved at that frame.

Now I'll write up the full plan with the worktree setup, crate scaffolding, workspace integration, file-by-file breakdown, verification phases, and open questions—but I won't execute anything yet since the user asked for a proposal.

content.data:
I've inspected the codebase. Before laying out the plan, here's what's relevant.

**What you have today**

The semi-persistent Vec lives in `containers/src/vec.rs` and is built on three pieces:

- `Vec<T, I, S, const TRACK: bool>` — the public façade with `push/pop/get/set/len/mark/restore` (`containers/src/vec.rs:25`).
- `DiffStore<T, I, TRACK>` — storage trait with two impls (`containers/src/diff_store.rs:11`):
  - `ParallelStore<T, I>` — separate bitset for "captured" flags. Works for any `T: Clone`. *Easiest to verify.*
  - `InlineStore<T: Tagged, I>` — capture flag stolen from MSB of `T::Repr`. *Harder to verify (bit packing).*
- `VecToken` / `Frame` / `ForkHistory` / `ContainerId` — token validation across nested marks and abandoned branches (`containers/src/token.rs`).

The behavioral oracle already exists informally as proptests in `containers/tests/vec_proptest.rs`: maintain a `std::Vec<u32>` alongside, snapshot it at every `mark`, and assert equality after every `pop`/`get`/`restore`. That's exactly the spec we want to lift to Verus.

**What "Verus-friendly" looks like in this repo**

`abstract-domains` is the existing precedent: pinned Verus version (`abstract-domains/.verus-version` → `0.2026.04.12.f1166c4`), per-crate `rust-toolchain.toml`, `vstd` dependency, `[package.metadata.verus] verify = true`, excluded from the default `cargo build`, and built via `cargo verus verify`. CI: `.github/workflows/verus.yml`. We'll mirror that exactly.

---

# Proposal

## 1. Worktree + branch

```
git worktree add ../semi-persistent-containers-verus -b verify/containers
```

Naming follows the existing pattern (`semi-persistent-au`, `semi-persistent-proof-search`, etc.). The branch is `verify/containers` because we expect this work to extend beyond just `Vec` over time.

## 2. New subcrate: `containers-verus/`

Layout:

```
containers-verus/
├── Cargo.toml                 # publish=false, vstd dep, [package.metadata.verus] verify=true
├── rust-toolchain.toml        # pin to "1.94.0" like abstract-domains
├── .verus-version             # pinned Verus release
├── .gitignore                 # /target, .verus-solver-log
├── README.md                  # what's verified, how to run
├── verify-all.sh              # mirrors abstract-domains/verify-all.sh, per-module
├── doc/
│   └── proof-status.md        # running tally of lemmas/admits per module
└── src/
    ├── lib.rs                 # module declarations, no_std-ish prelude
    ├── seq_ops.rs             # spec helpers over vstd::seq::Seq
    ├── index_like.rs          # IndexLike spec'd as a trait or monomorphic u32
    ├── diff_log.rs            # spec of "replay diff log in reverse on a Seq"
    ├── parallel_store.rs      # ParallelStore<T> + invariants + lemmas
    ├── frame.rs               # Frame + frame-stack invariants
    ├── fork_history.rs        # ForkHistory + token-validity proofs
    ├── vec.rs                 # Vec built on ParallelStore — the headline proof
    └── inline_store.rs        # InlineStore — Phase 2, see below
```

### Workspace integration

Add `containers-verus` to the workspace `members` list, but exclude it from the default flow exactly like `abstract-domains`:

- Update `README.md` and `.kiro/steering/product.md` exclusion notes.
- Update `cargo test --workspace --exclude semi-persistent-abstract-domains` → also `--exclude semi-persistent-containers-verus`.
- Extend `.github/workflows/verus.yml` matrix to verify both crates (or split into two jobs sharing the Verus install step).

`Cargo.toml` skeleton:

```toml
[package]
name = "semi-persistent-containers-verus"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"
publish = false
autobins = false

[dependencies]
vstd = "=0.0.0-2026-04-12-0118"   # match abstract-domains pin

[package.metadata.verus]
verify = true

[lints.rust]
unexpected_cfgs = { level = "warn", check-cfg = ['cfg(verus_only)'] }
```

## 3. What we actually prove — phased

Verus has rough edges with const-generics and traits, so I propose verifying a **monomorphized port** rather than re-engineering the existing generic surface. The original crate stays as the production code; `containers-verus` is the model + proof.

### Phase 1 — `ParallelStore`-backed `Vec<u32>` (the headline result)

This is the cleanest target: `T = u32`, `I = u32`, no `Tagged` bit-stealing, just a `Vec<u32>` for data and a `Vec<bool>` (or `Seq<bool>`) for captured flags.

**Spec view:**

```rust
spec fn view(&self) -> Seq<u32>             // the abstract sequence
spec fn frame_view(&self, k: nat) -> Seq<u32>   // the abstract sequence at the k-th mark
```

**Invariants (the heart of the proof):**

1. **Length consistency:** `view().len() == self.store.len()`.
2. **Capture bit honesty:** for every slot `i < saved_len(top frame)`, `captured[i]` ⇔ "an entry for `i` exists in `diff_log[top.diff_start..]`".
3. **First-write-wins:** for every frame `f` and every index `i`, the entries in `diff_log[f.diff_start..f_next.diff_start]` with second component `i` form a sequence of length ≤ 1.
4. **Frame replay invariant (the key one):**

   ```
   forall k in 0..frames.len():
       frame_view(k) == replay_reverse(view(), diff_log[frames[k].diff_start..]).truncate(frames[k].saved_len)
   ```

   In words: replaying the diff log backwards over the current state yields the state at the `k`-th mark.

**Theorems (per public method):**

- `push(v)` — `view'() == view().push(v)`, all frame views unchanged.
- `pop()` — `view'() == view().drop_last()`, frame views unchanged.
- `get(i)` — returns `view()[i]`.
- `set(i, v)` — `view'() == view().update(i, v)`, frame views unchanged.
- `mark()` — `view'() == view()`, `frames'.last().view == view()`.
- `restore(t)` — `view'() == frame_view(t.frame_index)` *at the time the token was issued*.

The frame-replay invariant is what makes `restore` correct in one shot.

### Phase 2 — `InlineStore` (the bit-stealing version)

Model `T::Repr` as a pair `(value: u32, tag: bool)` at the spec level (Verus' `Ghost` is your friend). Re-prove the same theorems with the additional bookkeeping that `prepare_mark` clears tags exactly in `[0..saved_len]`.

This is meaningfully harder because the capture flag and the value share a word; we need a refinement showing the executable bit-packed version simulates the ghost pair.

### Phase 3 — `ForkHistory` token validity

This is a small standalone state machine. Prove:

- A fresh token from `mark()` is `is_valid` immediately after.
- After `restore(t)` and another `mark()`, tokens from the abandoned future are `!is_valid` (no false negatives).
- Tokens from a strict ancestor branch on the current path are `is_valid` (no false positives that would let a stale token through).

This is independent of the storage proofs and can be done in parallel.

### Phase 4 — Other containers (out of scope for the initial branch)

Once `Vec` is proved, the same pattern lifts to `AppendOnlyVec` (trivial: restore = truncate), `Map`, `SparseSet`, `ListArena`, `BPlusTreeSet`. We won't tackle these yet.

## 4. Engineering details and tradeoffs to flag

- **No drop-in source reuse.** The verified version will be a parallel implementation, not a `verus!` overlay on `containers/src/vec.rs`. The original crate uses `std::vec::Vec`, generic `IndexLike`, atomic counters for `ContainerId`, and trait dispatch — all things Verus handles poorly today. Cleaner to write a Verus-native version that's behaviorally equivalent and prove it, then leave a comment in `containers/src/vec.rs` pointing at the proof.
- **`std::vec::Vec` vs `vstd::vec::Vec`.** Use `vstd::vec::Vec` (or `Seq` at the spec layer) inside the verified crate.
- **Const generic `TRACK`.** Don't try to verify both `TRACK=true` and `TRACK=false` at once. Verify `TRACK=true` (the interesting case) and trust that `TRACK=false` is a compile-time noop by inspection.
- **`ContainerId`.** The atomic counter is impure. Model it as a ghost `nat` parameter at construction time; that's enough to express "tokens from container A don't validate on container B".
- **Performance assertions.** Don't try to prove the *complexity* (O(1) mark, O(k) restore) in Verus — that's a static cost analysis the prover doesn't help with. Stick to functional correctness.
- **Existing tests stay.** `containers/tests/vec_proptest.rs` is the differential check; if the verified port and the production `Vec` ever diverge in behavior, proptests will catch it. We can add a translation harness later.

## 5. First milestone

A single PR/commit on `verify/containers` containing:

1. The new crate with empty modules and CI green (Verus verifies a no-op `lib.rs`).
2. `parallel_store.rs` fully proved: `push`, `pop`, `get`, `set`, `truncate` plus the capture protocol.
3. `vec.rs` with `mark`/`restore` proved for **single-frame** (no nesting). Nested marks come in milestone 2.

That's a tractable first chunk and gets the proof harness, CI, and Cargo plumbing all working before tackling the harder invariants.

---

**Open questions before I start executing:**

1. Branch name `verify/containers` or do you prefer `feature/containers-verus`?
2. Worktree directory `../semi-persistent-containers-verus` (matches existing convention) or somewhere else?
3. Phase 1 scope: just `ParallelStore` + single-frame `mark/restore`, or push to nested marks in milestone 1?
4. Do you want me to also extend the Verus CI workflow now, or land that in a follow-up?

If you confirm those, I'll create the worktree, scaffold the crate, wire up the workspace, and put a stub `vec.rs` in place that compiles under Verus before we start adding lemmas.

data.text:
The user's pushing back on monomorphization, and they have a point—the interesting parts really do involve the trait system around `Tagged`, `IndexLike`, and `DiffStore`. I should reconsider what Verus can actually handle with generics and traits right now, especially around const generics and trait bounds like `Clone`.

Looking at Verus's actual capabilities, generic types with trait bounds are supported, spec methods in traits work, and const generics have been improving. The key realization is that `Tagged` can be modeled as a verified trait with spec methods for viewing the underlying value and the tag itself, plus refinement requirements that prove the bit-stealing version is functionally equivalent to a straightforward representation. This is where the real verification challenge lies—not in the generics, but in proving the `Tagged` contract preserves the necessary invariants.

The same approach applies to `IndexLike` as a bijection with successor operations, and `DiffStore` as an abstract sequence with method contracts. The actual payoff is verifying the generic abstraction polymorphically—proving each method of the main vector type works for any trait implementation, which is where real bugs would hide. So the revised plan is to specify the trait contracts formally with open spec functions and axioms, then verify the vector implementation against those specs generically, and finally prove that both concrete stores satisfy their contracts.

One wrinkle is whether Verus handles const generics like `TRACK: bool` cleanly; if not, I could either use an associated const or split into separate `TrackedVec` and `UntrackedVec` types.

For the `TRACK=false` case, there's really nothing to prove since it's just a compile-time elision—I can verify the `TRACK=true` version and document that the untracked variant is trivially correct by inspection. On the `ContainerId` and atomics front, I can ghost-model the atomic counter without needing to verify the atomic itself. I also need to be careful with `Clone` bounds in Verus, leaning toward `Copy` or using Verus's updated `Clone` support, and I'll rely on `vstd::vec::Vec` for the verified vector operations.

The real payoff here is that verifying the generic trait system itself—rather than just one instantiation—makes the whole proof obligation fall on the trait specs for `Tagged`, `IndexLike`, and `DiffStore`.

The spec has solidified around const generics with `TRACK: bool` now natively supported. When `TRACK = false`, containers behave identically to their standard library equivalents with no mark/restore functionality, while `TRACK = true` enables the semi-persistent tracking behavior.

For the specification design, I'm defining a uniform view function that works across both modes, then adding ghost-level specs for the tracking case—a ghost stack holding deep copies at each mark frame and a fork tree tracking valid tokens. The ensures clauses for operations like `push`, `set`, and `mark` will specify how the view and ghost stack evolve, with `mark` returning a token that captures the current state in the stack.

The trickiest part is modeling branch validity: when we restore to frame k, all frames beyond it get "cut off" and their tokens become invalid. I'm representing this as a tree where each node is a frame with its saved view, and the current path tracks which nodes are reachable from root to head. A token is valid only if its node lies on the current path, which cleanly captures the branching semantics without losing history—the nodes stay append-only so we can always walk back through abandoned branches if needed.

The executable `ForkHistory` optimizes this by storing just the current branch ID and a chain of parent references, then validates tokens by walking up that chain. I need to prove this walking algorithm correctly computes whether a token's node is on the current path, using a refinement mapping from branch-depth pairs to abstract node IDs.

For the `TRACK = false` case, I can use separate impl blocks gated on the const generic: one impl provides only the basic operations (push, pop, get, set) without mark/restore, while the other impl with `TRACK = true` includes the full machinery. The current code uses an assertion in mark/restore, which I'd verify as a precondition. When `TRACK = false`, the vector should be equivalent to a regular Vec since the diff log becomes irrelevant.

Now I'm organizing how to present this: I'll lay out the overall spec with `view() : Seq<T>` always present, then show two correctness theorems side by side—one for `TRACK = false` matching standard Vec semantics, and one for `TRACK = true` that additionally tracks the ghost stack and fork tree. The trickiest part is the branch safety theorem, where I need to define the ForkTree as an append-only structure with a current path, make token validity depend on being on that path, and prove the executable fork history walking algorithm computes validity correctly as a refinement.

For the safety property itself, I'm thinking the cleaner approach is a precondition: `restore(t)` requires `is_valid(t)`, so calling it with an invalid token becomes a Verus type error—production code's panic is provably unreachable. This shifts the proof burden to callers, who must establish that their tokens are valid. The contract is straightforward: `mark()` returns a valid token and preserves validity of all prior tokens on the current path, while `restore(t)` requires validity and invalidates tokens from any cut subtree.

I should also clarify that tokens are just copyable structs with state-dependent validity predicates tied to the vector's current state, not linear resources. And I need to account for the `ContainerId` check in the precondition to prevent cross-container token misuse.

For the other container types like Map, SparseSet, and BPlusTreeSet, each has its own abstract spec that the ghost stack tracks — storing a sequence of snapshots at each mark frame. When `TRACK=false`, the equivalence to standard types is trivial since we never deviate from the normal behavior.

The real insight is that instead of proving the algorithmic property of replaying diffs, I can just prove that after restore, the view matches the saved ghost snapshot — much simpler. The challenge is maintaining the ghost stack correctly across all operations, particularly ensuring mark pushes a deep copy and that push/pop/set operations update it properly.

For restore, the proof obligation is maintaining the invariant that replaying the diff log from any frame recovers the corresponding ghost snapshot. I should also handle the const-generic precondition for mark/restore — either through a runtime assertion or by splitting into separate implementations. The ghost state itself needs a sequence of sequences for the deep-copy stack and a fork tree for the validity predicate.

content.data:
Good — that simplifies the const-generic story and lets us write the spec the way it should be written. Your phrasing also gives us a much cleaner proof than the "replay-the-diff-log-equals-frame-state" formulation I was reaching for. Let me sketch what this looks like.

## The two-mode specification

Every container gets the same shape:

```rust
verus! {
pub struct Vec<T, I: IndexLike, S: DiffStore<T, I, TRACK>, const TRACK: bool> {
    // exec fields:
    store: S,
    diff_log: vstd::vec::Vec<(T, I)>,
    frames: vstd::vec::Vec<Frame>,
    forks: ForkHistory,
    id: ContainerId,

    // ghost fields (zero runtime cost, present only when TRACK):
    snapshots: Ghost<Seq<Seq<T>>>,   // deep copies, one per active frame
    fork_tree: Ghost<ForkTree>,      // append-only history of all marks
}

impl<T, I, S, const TRACK: bool> Vec<T, I, S, TRACK> {
    pub closed spec fn view(&self) -> Seq<T>;       // always defined
    pub closed spec fn snapshots(&self) -> Seq<Seq<T>>;  // empty when !TRACK
    pub closed spec fn wf(&self) -> bool;
}
}
```

`view()` is the abstract value (a `Seq<T>`). `snapshots()` is the ghost stack of deep copies. The well-formedness predicate `wf()` ties them to the exec fields.

### Theorem A — when `TRACK = false`, we behave like `std::Vec`

This becomes a one-liner per method, because there's literally nothing to track:

```rust
exec fn push(&mut self, value: T)
    requires old(self).wf()
    ensures
        self.wf(),
        self.view() == old(self).view().push(value),
        // tracking is inert:
        TRACK == false ==> self.snapshots() == Seq::<Seq<T>>::empty(),
        TRACK == false ==> self.frames@.len() == 0,
        TRACK == false ==> self.diff_log@.len() == 0;
```

Adding `mark` and `restore` with `requires TRACK == true` makes them statically uncallable on a `Vec<_, _, _, false>`. (Equivalently, we split into two `impl` blocks; same outcome.) So the `TRACK = false` correctness theorem is just: *for every public exec method, `view()` evolves like `Seq<T>`*. Identical reasoning applies to `Map<K,V>` ↔ `vstd::map::Map<K,V>`, `SparseSet<T>` ↔ `Set<T>`, `BPlusTreeSet<T>` ↔ `Set<T>`, etc.

This is the "free" half of the proof. The expensive half is `TRACK = true`.

### Theorem B — when `TRACK = true`, restore matches the deep copy

The ghost stack `snapshots` is the explicit form of what was implicit before. Each method's contract maintains it:

| Method | `view()` | `snapshots()` | `fork_tree` |
|---|---|---|---|
| `push(v)` | `view().push(v)` | unchanged | unchanged |
| `pop()` | `view().drop_last()` | unchanged | unchanged |
| `set(i, v)` | `view().update(i, v)` | unchanged | unchanged |
| `mark()` → `t` | unchanged | `snapshots().push(view())` | extends current path with new node, returns token for it |
| `restore(t)` | `snapshots()[t.frame_idx]` | truncated to `t.frame_idx` | current path truncated to `t.node` |

The only nontrivial proof is `restore`, where we have to show the executable diff-log replay actually produces `snapshots()[t.frame_idx]`. The shape of that proof:

**Frame invariant** (part of `wf()`):

```rust
spec fn frame_inv(&self) -> bool {
    forall|k: int| 0 <= k < self.frames@.len() ==>
        self.snapshots()[k] ==
            replay_reverse(
                self.view(),
                self.diff_log@.subrange(self.frames@[k].diff_start as int,
                                        self.diff_log@.len() as int)
            ).subrange(0, self.frames@[k].saved_len as int)
}
```

Read: the deep copy at frame `k` equals "replay the suffix of the diff log past frame `k` over the current view, then truncate to the saved length". Push/pop/set each preserve this with a small lemma. `mark` extends the seq by one with `view()` as the new top, trivial. `restore(t)` *uses* the invariant to discharge its postcondition — the executable replay-and-truncate machinery in `vec.rs:restore` is exactly what the spec says, so the postcondition `view() == snapshots()[t.frame_idx]` falls out by unrolling.

This is the same heart-of-the-proof we'd have had to do anyway, but framed as "the ghost stack is faithfully represented by the diff log" rather than as a freestanding algorithmic property. Cleaner.

## Branch-cut safety: the fork-tree spec

This is where your second requirement lives. The ghost model is an append-only tree:

```rust
verus! {
pub ghost struct ForkTree {
    pub nodes: Seq<ForkNode>,           // never shrinks; node id = index
    pub current_path: Seq<int>,         // root-to-head, sequence of node ids
}

pub ghost struct ForkNode {
    pub parent: Option<int>,
    pub created_at_depth: nat,          // for sanity
    pub saved_view: Seq<T>,             // ghost deep copy at this node
}

pub closed spec fn is_valid(self, t: VecToken) -> bool {
    self.current_path.contains(t.node_id)
        && t.container_id == self.container_id
}
}
```

`mark()` appends a new node whose parent is the head of `current_path`, then extends `current_path` by that node id. Old current_path is a *prefix* of the new one ⇒ all previously valid tokens stay valid.

`restore(t)` requires `is_valid(t)` and **truncates** `current_path` so it ends at `t.node_id`. Any node id that was on the old path past `t` is no longer in the new path. Tokens pointing at those nodes are now invalid — that's the cut.

A subsequent `mark()` extends `current_path` with a fresh node id that's *different* from the cut node ids (because `nodes` is append-only — we never reuse ids). So tokens for the abandoned subtree never come back to life.

### The contract that gives you safety

```rust
exec fn restore(&mut self, t: VecToken)
    requires
        old(self).wf(),
        old(self).fork_tree@.is_valid(t),     // <-- can't restore a cut token
        TRACK == true,
    ensures
        self.wf(),
        self.view() == old(self).snapshots()[t.frame_idx as int],
        self.snapshots() == old(self).snapshots().subrange(0, t.frame_idx as int + 1),
        self.fork_tree@.current_path
            == old(self).fork_tree@.current_path.subrange(0, /* index of t.node */);
```

Because `is_valid` is a precondition, the *caller* must prove the token is on the current path. After a `restore` cuts a subtree, no ambient knowledge will let the caller re-prove validity for tokens in that subtree — the path simply doesn't contain them. The Verus type checker rejects the call. The production code's `assert!(self.forks.is_valid(...))` becomes provably unreachable.

You don't have to give up tokens being `Copy` to get this. Tokens are forgeable by hand, sure, but a hand-forged token can't satisfy `is_valid` because `is_valid` references a ghost that only this `Vec` can extend.

### The executable `ForkHistory` is a refinement

The production `ForkHistory` doesn't store a tree — it stores a `current_branch_id` and a list of `(parent_branch_id, fork_depth)` origins, and walks up the chain in `is_valid`. We prove this is a faithful refinement of the ghost tree:

```rust
spec fn refines(exec: ForkHistory, ghost: ForkTree) -> bool {
    // bijection between (branch_id, depth) pairs reachable in exec
    // and node ids on ghost.current_path or its abandoned siblings, such that
    // exec.is_valid(token) <==> ghost.current_path.contains(map(token)).
}
```

Then `exec_fn ForkHistory::is_valid` gets `ensures result == ghost.current_path.contains(node_id)`, and that's enough to discharge the precondition on `restore`.

This refinement proof is medium-effort but stand-alone — fork-history doesn't depend on `Vec`'s internals at all, so we can verify it in parallel with the storage proofs.

### `ContainerId`

`ContainerId::new()` reads an atomic counter; we wrap it `external_body` and ghost-axiomatize "every call returns a value distinct from all previously-returned values". That's enough to prove `t.container_id == self.id` is unforgeable across containers.

## Updated milestones

- **M1.** Trait specs for `Tagged`, `IndexLike`, `DiffStore` (with the new ghost-deep-copy formulation).
- **M2.** `ParallelStore<T, I>` + `InlineStore<T, I>` impls proved against `DiffStore`.
- **M3.** `Vec<T, I, S, TRACK>` proved for both modes:
  - `TRACK=false`: equivalence to `Seq<T>` semantics, no ghost machinery.
  - `TRACK=true`: ghost-snapshot stack invariant, single-frame mark/restore.
- **M4.** Nested marks + the full `frame_inv`.
- **M5.** `ForkTree` ghost spec + `ForkHistory` refinement + `ContainerId` ghost identity. Branch-cut safety lands here.
- **M6.** Replicate the same spec shape for `AppendOnlyVec`, `Map`, `SparseSet`, `BPlusTreeSet`, `ListArena`. Each gets its own `view()` (a `Seq`/`Map`/`Set`) and a corresponding ghost-snapshot stack; the fork-tree machinery is shared.

The fork-tree spec and the deep-copy stack are both reusable across every container, so M5 and the bookkeeping in M6 amortize across the workspace.

## What I'd want to confirm before scaffolding

1. The ghost-snapshot stack stores `Seq<Seq<T>>` for `Vec<T>`, `Seq<Map<K,V>>` for `Map<K,V>`, etc. Each container's ghost type matches its abstract spec. OK with you?
2. Branch-cut safety as a *precondition* on `restore` (Verus rejects the call) versus as a postcondition (`Result<(), TokenInvalid>`). Precondition is the cleaner spec and matches the existing `assert!`. I'd go with precondition unless you want the typed-error variant for some interop reason.
3. `Tagged`'s contract ought to be strong enough that the ghost-snapshot stack for `InlineStore` is just `Seq<Seq<T>>` (the `T` view, with tags stripped) — no need to expose `T::Repr` in the spec. Sound to you?
4. Same branch/worktree as before (`verify/containers` at `../semi-persistent-containers-verus`)?

Confirm those four and I'll create the worktree, scaffold the crate, and get an empty `lib.rs` through Verus before we start writing any of the trait specs.

Scaffold landed on `containers-verus` (commit `5736611`).

**Summary**

- Worktree: `/Users/delmasrd/projects/containers-verus`, branch `containers-verus` (matching name as requested).

content.data:
## Where we stand

The other agent moved M1 forward but the work is uncommitted and incomplete in important places. Verification passes (48 verified, 0 errors), but "passes" is doing a lot of heavy lifting because some of the critical contracts are too weak to prove anything downstream.

### What was done (uncommitted, in your working tree)

**`tagged.rs` — trait + primitive impls.** Solid first pass. `Tagged` exposes `value_of` / `tag_of` ghost projections and five exec methods (`into_repr`, `from_repr`, `tag`, `set_tag`, `clear_tag`) with contracts that compose into the round-trip property we wanted. Concrete impls for `u8/u16/u32/u64/usize` use a new `BoolPair<T>` struct as the repr.

**`index_like.rs` — trait + primitive impls.** Mostly good. `as_nat` / `max_nat` / `zero_spec` ghost projections, three lemma obligations (bounded, injective, zero), three exec methods (`zero`, `as_usize`, `try_from_usize`).

**`diff_store.rs` — trait shape only.** All methods declared with reasonable signatures, but the capture-protocol contracts are stubs.

**`verify-all.sh`** — narrowed to the three modules that have content.

### Issues you should know about

1. **The niche obligation is missing from `Tagged`.** This was your explicit guidance — bit-stealing impls must prove the type "accepts a niche". The current trait has no `repr_wf` predicate, no encode/decode bijection axiom, nothing tying value-bit-width to repr-bit-width. The `BoolPair` impls work because they don't need a niche at all (they pay an extra word per slot). When we go to write the `DenseId<31>` over `u32` impl — the actual reason for this trait's existence — there's no contract slot to discharge the niche proof into. **This needs to be added before we write any bit-stealing impl, otherwise the proof is unsound by omission.**

2. **`DiffStore`'s capture-protocol contracts are too weak to use.** Specifically:
   - `capture(i, saved_len, log)` ensures only `wf()` and `data() == old.data()`. It says nothing about *first-write-wins* — no postcondition on `captured` or `diff_log`. An impl that just returns immediately without doing anything would satisfy this.
   - `restore_entry` ensures only `wf()`. It says nothing about how `data` evolves. An impl that ignores `old_value` would satisfy this.
   - `finish_restore` doesn't specify what `captured` becomes.

   These need real ensures clauses before `Vec`'s `restore` proof can be written. M1 is not actually done on `DiffStore` — it's a stubbed-out trait shape.

3. **`u64::as_usize` truncates on 32-bit hosts.** `as_usize` for `u64` is `external_body` and `self as usize`, with contract `r as nat == self.as_nat()`. On a 32-bit host this is unsound (the cast wraps). Fine in practice for our 64-bit targets but worth a comment or a `cfg` guard.

4. **`IndexLike` is missing `Ord` and `MIN`/`MAX` constants.** The production trait has `MIN`/`MAX` and orderability, used in places like `active_saved_len: I` with comparison. We'll need these for `Vec`.

### Junk to clean up before committing

- `containers-verus/liblib.rlib` — stray Verus output, ~150KB.
- `containers-verus/.claude/` — IDE settings the agent left behind.
- `session-2026-05-20.json` at the worktree root — looks like a Kiro session export (667KB). Probably not meant to be tracked.

None of these are in `.gitignore`. I'd add them (or at least `liblib.rlib` and `.claude/`) and remove the files.

### Recommendation

Before continuing to M2 (`ParallelStore`/`InlineStore` impls), I'd:

1. Add the niche-proof obligation to `Tagged` (the `repr_wf` + bijection axioms we discussed). Otherwise, the first bit-stealing impl will have to retrofit them.
2. Strengthen the `DiffStore` contracts so `capture`/`restore_entry`/`finish_restore` actually pin down behavior. Without those, M2 will be writing impls that satisfy a vacuous spec.
3. Clean up the working tree and commit the M1 work as-is (or with the fixes above, depending on how aggressive you want to be in one commit).

Want me to do (1) and (2), then run verify again, then commit?

content.data:
## Where we stand

Big progress. Two new commits since my last review:

```
3e595bd  Land M2: ParallelStore and InlineStore impls (verified)
208fc38  Land M1 trait specs: Tagged, IndexLike, DiffStore
5736611  Scaffold containers-verus subcrate
```

**105 verified, 0 errors.** Per-module: tagged 31, index_like 42, diff_store 0 (just contracts, no bodies), parallel_store 16, inline_store 16.

### M1 — addresses every issue I flagged

- **Niche obligation in `Tagged`.** `repr_wf` predicate added; `lemma_repr_extensional` axiom requires impls to prove that two well-formed reprs with equal `(value_of, tag_of)` are equal. That's the niche-injectivity property — without it the encoding could waste state-space and verify vacuously. `BoolPair` impls discharge it as `repr_wf := true`. The slot is there for `DenseId`'s bit-stealing impl when we add it.
- **`DiffStore` contracts are now real.** `capture` pins down first-write-wins (diff log + `captured` deltas conditional on `i < saved_len && !captured[i]`). `restore_entry` specifies overwrite-vs-push behavior. `finish_restore` pins down the rebuilt `captured` as an existential over surviving diff entries. An impl can no longer satisfy these with no-ops.
- **`u64::as_usize` truncation.** Now gated behind `#[cfg(target_pointer_width = "64")]`.
- **`IndexLike` ordering + min/max.** `min_spec`/`max_spec` and `lt_spec`/`le_spec` added with corresponding exec methods. Production parity.

### M2 — `ParallelStore` and `InlineStore` both verified

Both impls are complete with real bodies, including the two non-trivial loops (`prepare_mark`'s clear-all-tags, `finish_restore`'s clear-then-rebuild from diff suffix). The loop invariants on `finish_restore` are non-obvious — the existential over `kk < k` reconstructing `captured[j]` from processed-so-far diffs is the right shape. `InlineStore` proves its `wf` (every repr is `T::repr_wf`) is preserved across all mutating methods, which is the payoff from the M1 niche obligation.

One small caveat: `shrink_if` is a no-op in both impls. Production performs capacity reclamation; the verified version intentionally skips it because `shrink_to` doesn't change the abstract `@` view. Documented in the source. Acceptable tradeoff but worth noting if benchmarks come up later.

### Still empty / to do

- `frame.rs`, `fork_history.rs`, `container_id.rs`, `vec.rs` — all stubs.
- M3 (single-frame `Vec`), M4 (nested marks + `frame_inv`), M5 (fork-tree + branch-cut safety) untouched.

### Working-tree hygiene

Three things not in `.gitignore`:

1. `containers-verus/.verus-log/` — ~18 MB of Verus debug artifacts (call graphs, SMT transcripts, VIR dumps). Should not be tracked. Add `.verus-log/` to the crate's `.gitignore`.
2. `session-2026-05-20.json` at the worktree root — 668 KB Kiro session dump. Either delete or add to root `.gitignore`. Currently shows as untracked.
3. `liblib.rlib` is gitignored fine (✓).

Want me to fix the `.gitignore` and delete the stale `liblib.rlib` + `.verus-log/` so the working tree is clean before M3 starts?

content.data:
#!/bin/bash
set +e
# Find a timeout command (GNU `timeout` is named `gtimeout` on macOS via coreutils).
if command -v timeout >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  TIMEOUT=timeout
elif command -v gtimeout >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  TIMEOUT=gtimeout
else
  TIMEOUT=""
fi

run_verus() {
  if [ -n "$TIMEOUT" ]; then
    $TIMEOUT 600 verus --trace "$@"
  else
    verus --trace "$@"
  fi
}

FAIL=0
for mod in nats tnum anum bools tbit chopped exec_tnum div unum domains::d8 domains::d16 domains::d32 domains::d64; do
  printf "%-20s " "$mod:"
  output=$(run_verus src/lib.rs --crate-type=lib --verify-module "$mod" 2>&1)
  status=$?
  result=$(echo "$output" | grep "verification results" | head -1)
  if [ $status -eq 124 ]; then
    echo "TIMEOUT"
    FAIL=1
  elif [ -z "$result" ]; then
    echo "ERROR (exit $status)"
    echo "$output" | tail -20
    FAIL=1
  elif echo "$result" | grep -q "0 errors"; then
    echo "$result"
  else
    echo "FAIL: $result"
    echo "--- full output for $mod ---"
    echo "$output"
    echo "--- end output for $mod ---"
    echo "--- re-running $mod with --expand-errors ---"
    run_verus src/lib.rs --crate-type=lib --verify-module "$mod" --expand-errors 2>&1
    echo "--- end expand-errors for $mod ---"
    FAIL=1
  fi
done
exit $FAIL

/target

[toolchain]
channel = "1.94.0"

name: Verus Verification
on:
  pull_request:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  contents: read

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  verify:
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    timeout-minutes: 60
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          - os: ubuntu-24.04
            verus-asset: verus-x86-linux
          - os: macos-latest
            verus-asset: verus-arm64-macos
    defaults:
      run:
        shell: bash
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd # v6.0.2
        with:
          persist-credentials: false

      - name: Read pinned Verus version
        id: version
        run: echo "verus=$(cat abstract-domains/.verus-version)" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Download Verus
        id: verus
        env:
          VERUS_VERSION: ${{ steps.version.outputs.verus }}
          VERUS_ASSET: ${{ matrix.verus-asset }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          ASSET_URL="https://github.com/verus-lang/verus/releases/download/release%2F${VERUS_VERSION}/verus-${VERUS_VERSION}-${VERUS_ASSET#verus-}.zip"
          echo "Downloading: $ASSET_URL"
          curl -fL --retry 3 --retry-delay 2 -o /tmp/verus.zip "$ASSET_URL"
          unzip -q /tmp/verus.zip -d /tmp/verus-unpack
          rm /tmp/verus.zip
          # Move the single extracted directory
          mv /tmp/verus-unpack/verus-* ./verus
          # Detect the Rust toolchain Verus needs
          rust_toolchain=""
          if [ -f verus/version.json ]; then
            rust_toolchain="$(python3 -c 'import json; print(json.load(open("verus/version.json")).get("verus", {}).get("toolchain", ""))' 2>/dev/null || true)"
          fi
          if [ -z "$rust_toolchain" ]; then
            rust_toolchain="$(./verus/verus 2>&1 | grep -oE 'nightly-[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}' | head -n1 || true)"
          fi
          if [ -z "$rust_toolchain" ]; then
            echo "::error::Could not detect Verus Rust toolchain"
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "Detected Rust toolchain: $rust_toolchain"
          echo "rust_toolchain=$rust_toolchain" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Allow Verus through macOS Gatekeeper
        if: runner.os == 'macOS'
        run: |
          if [ -f verus/macos_allow_gatekeeper.sh ]; then
            bash verus/macos_allow_gatekeeper.sh || true
          fi

      - name: Setup Rust (Verus toolchain)
        env:
          VERUS_RUST_TOOLCHAIN: ${{ steps.verus.outputs.rust_toolchain }}
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          rustup toolchain install "$VERUS_RUST_TOOLCHAIN" --profile minimal --no-self-update
          rustup default "$VERUS_RUST_TOOLCHAIN"

      - name: Add Verus to PATH
        run: echo "${{ github.workspace }}/verus" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"

      - uses: Swatinem/rust-cache@e18b497796c12c097a38f9edb9d0641fb99eee32 # v2
        with:
          workspaces: abstract-domains

      - name: Verify abstract-domains
        working-directory: abstract-domains
        run: |
          verus --version
          cargo verus verify -- --trace

  # abstract-domains fuzz tests run with regular stable rustc
  fuzz-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd # v6.0.2
        with:
          persist-credentials: false
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-rust
      - uses: Swatinem/rust-cache@e18b497796c12c097a38f9edb9d0641fb99eee32 # v2
      - run: cargo test -p semi-persistent-abstract-domains

items.Text:
#!/bin/bash
set +e
# Find a timeout command (GNU `timeout` is named `gtimeout` on macOS via coreutils).
if command -v timeout >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  TIMEOUT=timeout
elif command -v gtimeout >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  TIMEOUT=gtimeout
else
  TIMEOUT=""
fi

run_verus() {
  if [ -n "$TIMEOUT" ]; then
    $TIMEOUT 600 verus --trace "$@"
  else
    verus --trace "$@"
  fi
}

FAIL=0
for mod in nats tnum anum bools tbit chopped exec_tnum div unum domains::d8 domains::d16 domains::d32 domains::d64; do
  printf "%-20s " "$mod:"
  output=$(run_verus src/lib.rs --crate-type=lib --verify-module "$mod" 2>&1)
  status=$?
  result=$(echo "$output" | grep "verification results" | head -1)
  if [ $status -eq 124 ]; then
    echo "TIMEOUT"
    FAIL=1
  elif [ -z "$result" ]; then
    echo "ERROR (exit $status)"
    echo "$output" | tail -20
    FAIL=1
  elif echo "$result" | grep -q "0 errors"; then
    echo "$result"
  else
    echo "FAIL: $result"
    echo "--- full output for $mod ---"
    echo "$output"
    echo "--- end output for $mod ---"
    echo "--- re-running $mod with --expand-errors ---"
    run_verus src/lib.rs --crate-type=lib --verify-module "$mod" --expand-errors 2>&1
    echo "--- end expand-errors for $mod ---"
    FAIL=1
  fi
done
exit $FAIL

content.data:
# Chapter 2 — Semi-Persistent Vectors

[← Ch 1: Dense IDs and Tagged](01-dense-ids-and-tagged.md) · [Table of Contents](00-table-of-contents.md) · [Ch 3: AppendOnlyVec →](03-append-only-vec.md)


## High-Level Idea

Many search algorithms need backtracking: explore a branch, then undo
everything and try a different branch. E-graph equality saturation,
SAT solving, constraint propagation, and game-tree search all share
this pattern. Backtracking requires snapshotting the entire mutable
state and restoring it later.

A naive clone-on-snapshot is O(n) per mark. The semi-persistent
vector takes a different approach: it records negative diffs into a
stack. On `mark()`, the vector stores its current length and starts
logging negative diffs in a frame of its private diff stack. On each
mutation, before overwriting a slot, it pushes the old value into the
negative diff stack. On `restore()`, it replays the log in reverse to
restore captured values, then truncates back to the saved length.
This gives O(1) mark and O(k) restore, where k is the number of
mutations since `mark()`.

The tricky part is the first-write-wins protocol: each slot of the Vec
must be logged at most once per frame (logging the same slot twice wastes
space and the second log entry is useless because only the original value
matters). This requires a per-slot "already captured" flag, which is
exactly the tag bit from Chapter 1. For `DenseId` types stored in
`InlineStore`, the capture flag is the MSB, costing zero extra memory.

### Token Safety

A `mark()` returns an opaque `VecToken`. The caller hands this token
back to `restore()` to undo. But tokens can go stale: if you restore
past a mark and then mark again, you're on a new timeline, and old
tokens from the abandoned future must be rejected. The engine detects
this with two mechanisms:

- `ContainerId` is a globally unique id per `Vec` instance (from an
  atomic counter). Every token records which container it came from.
  `restore()` checks the token's container id matches, preventing
  cross-container bugs (e.g., using one container's token on a different
  container).

- `ForkHistory` is a field in each vector that tracks branch history
  defined by `mark()/restore()` invocations. Each token records a
  `branch_id` and `depth` within that branch. When you restore past
  a mark and re-mark, a new branch is created with a new `branch_id`,
  and the fork point is recorded. On `restore()`, the history is walked
  to verify the token belongs to the current branch or a direct ancestor;
  tokens from terminated sibling branches cause panics.

```rust
pub struct VecToken {
    branch_id: u32,
    depth: u32,
    frame_index: u32,
    container_id: ContainerId,
}
```

## Type Parameters

The vector is parameterized by two type-level concerns and one
compile-time flag:

```rust
pub struct Vec<T, I: IndexLike, S: DiffStore<T, I, TRACK>, const TRACK: bool>
```

`T` is the element type. When stored in an `InlineStore`, `T` must
implement `Tagged` so the capture bit can be packed into each slot's
representation. When stored in a `ParallelStore`, `T` only needs
`Clone` (the capture bits live in a separate `BitSet`).

`I: IndexLike` is the index type. It must admit a bijection to
`[0, N)` for some N determined by its bit width. Concretely,
`IndexLike` requires `to_usize` and `from_usize` conversions, plus
an associated `MAX` constant that bounds the addressable range. The
diff log stores `(I, T)` pairs, so a narrow index type (e.g., `u16`)
keeps diff entries compact.

`DiffStore<T, I, TRACK>` is the storage backend trait, parameterized
by both `T` and `I`. It defines the capture protocol (`capture`,
`prepare_mark`, `restore_entry`) and the raw data access (`get`,
`set_raw`, `push`, `truncate`). `InlineStore` and `ParallelStore`
are the two implementations.

The key unification: `DenseId` implements both `Tagged` and
`IndexLike`. A dense id can be stored in an `InlineStore` (using its
MSB as the capture flag) and simultaneously used as the index type
for a different vector. This is why a single
`VecI<MyId, MyId::Index, TRACK>` can serve as a parent-pointer array:
the elements are id values with zero-cost capture tracking, indexed
by the same id type.

Two convenience aliases capture the common cases:

```rust
type VecI<T: Tagged, I, TRACK> = Vec<T, I, InlineStore<T, I, TRACK>, TRACK>;
type VecP<T: Clone, I, TRACK>  = Vec<T, I, ParallelStore<T, I, TRACK>, TRACK>;
```

## API

| Operation | Cost | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| `push(val)` | O(1) amortized | Append element |
| `pop()` | O(1) | Remove last element |
| `get(i)` | O(1) | Read element at index |
| `view().set(i, val)` | O(1) | Write element (with capture) |
| `len()` | O(1) | Current length |
| `mark()` → `VecToken` | O(1) | Snapshot current state |
| `restore(token)` | O(k) | Undo all mutations since mark |

## The Diff-Log Protocol

### Mark

```
mark():
    push Frame { saved_len: current len, diff_start: diff_log.len() }
    store.prepare_mark(saved_len, &diff_log[prev_frame..])
```

On `InlineStore`, `prepare_mark` clears all tag bits in slots
`[0..saved_len]` so that subsequent mutations can be detected.

### Mutate

```
view().set(i, new_val):
    store.capture(i, saved_len, &mut diff_log)
    store.set_raw(i, new_val)
```

`capture` on `InlineStore`: if `i < saved_len` and tag bit is clear,
log `(old_value, i)` to diff_log and set the tag bit. If tag is
already set, the slot was already captured, so it skips. This ensures each
slot is logged at most once per frame.

`push(val)` just appends to the store; no capture is needed because
truncation on restore handles new elements.

### Restore

```
restore(token):
    frame = frames[token.frame_idx]
    for (old_val, idx) in diff_log[frame.diff_start..].rev():
        store.restore_entry(idx, &old_val, frame.saved_len)
    store.finish_restore(&diff_log[frame.diff_start..], frame.saved_len)
    diff_log.truncate(frame.diff_start)
    store.truncate(frame.saved_len)
    frames.truncate(token.frame_idx)
```

Replays the diff log in reverse order, restoring each modified slot
to the marked value corresponding to the token. Then truncates to the
saved length, removing any diff entries that were pushed after the mark.

## Nested Marks

Marks can be nested. Each `mark()` pushes a new `Frame`. `restore()`
pops back to the specified frame, undoing all intermediate frames.

```
mark()  → token_A (saved_len=10)
  push, push, set(3, x)     // diff_log: [(old_3, 3)]
  mark()  → token_B (saved_len=12)
    set(5, y)                // diff_log: [(old_3, 3), (old_5, 5)]
  restore(token_B)           // undo set(5,y), truncate to 12
restore(token_A)             // undo set(3,x), truncate to 10
```

## `InlineStore` — Zero-Overhead Tracking

```rust
pub struct InlineStore<T: Tagged, I: IndexLike, const TRACK: bool> {
    data: std::vec::Vec<T::Repr>,
}
```

The tag bit lives inside each `T::Repr`. For `DenseId` types, this is
the MSB of the `u32`, costing zero extra memory.

| Operation | Tag behavior |
|-----------|-------------|
| `push(val)` | Store `val.into_repr()` (tag=0) |
| `set_raw(i, val)` | Overwrite repr at `i` |
| `capture(i, ..)` | If `!tag(data[i])`: log old value, `set_tag(data[i])` |
| `prepare_mark(..)` | Clear all tags in `[0..saved_len]` |
| `restore_entry(i, old)` | `data[i] = old.into_repr()` |
| `get(i)` | `T::from_repr(&data[i])` (strips tag) |

## `ParallelStore` — For Non-Taggable Types

```rust
pub struct ParallelStore<T: Clone, I: IndexLike, const TRACK: bool> {
    data: std::vec::Vec<T>,
    bits: BitSet,
}
```

Same protocol, but the capture flag is a separate bit in `BitSet`.
Used for types like `(MyId, u32)` that don't implement `Tagged`.

## `View` — Mutable Accessor

`View` wraps `&mut Vec` and provides `get`/`set` with automatic
capture tracking, preventing accidental calls to `set_raw` without
capture:

## Token Validation

See "Token Safety" above for the full design. In summary:

- `ContainerId` prevents cross-container token use.
- `ForkHistory` prevents stale-branch token use.
- Both checks happen at the start of `restore()` and panic on
  violation; these are always programmer errors, never recoverable.

## `ShrinkPolicy` — Capacity Reclamation

Rust's `Vec::truncate` reduces `len` but preserves `capacity`. After
restoring from a large exploratory branch, the store's internal
arrays may have far more capacity than needed. Capacity ratchets
across branches, converging to the maximum branch size ever explored.

The original design principle: shrinking happens at mark time (before
the frame push), not at restore time. The rationale is that never
shrinking during restore avoids costly reallocations in tight
exploratory loops; the vec naturally "learns" the right capacity.
Shrinking is part of the maintenance phase inside `mark()`, alongside
resize and compaction — semantics-preserving transformations
that are not captured in the diff frames.

```rust
pub enum ShrinkPolicy {
    Never,
    IfOverallocated { factor: usize, headroom: usize },
}
```

With `IfOverallocated { factor, headroom }`, if
`capacity > factor * len`, the backing storage shrinks to
`headroom * len`. The same check is applied to the diff log.

`Never` is best for tight search loops with similar-sized branches.
`IfOverallocated` is for top-level marks after major search resets
where the previous branch was much larger than the next one will be.

All `mark()` methods across the container hierarchy accept a
`ShrinkPolicy`. All `restore()` methods take no policy — they just
undo. Applications typically store the policy as a configurable field
and propagate it through their top-level `mark()` to all
sub-containers.

---
[← Ch 1: Dense IDs and Tagged](01-dense-ids-and-tagged.md) · [Table of Contents](00-table-of-contents.md) · [Ch 3: AppendOnlyVec →](03-append-only-vec.md)

# Semi-Persistent Containers — Design Documents

Semi-persistent data structures with O(1) snapshots and O(k) restoration.
Building blocks for applications that need fast state snapshots and
backtracking — e-graph engines, SAT solvers, constraint propagators,
game-tree searchers, and similar systems.

All containers are built on top of a single core primitive: the
semi-persistent vector (Chapter 2), which uses a diff-log protocol
to record mutations and replay them in reverse on restore.

## Chapters

1. **[Dense Identifiers and the `Tagged` Trait](01-dense-ids-and-tagged.md)**
   How 31-bit ids steal a tag bit for inline capture tracking.
   `DenseId`, `Tagged`, `Opt<T>`, `BoolTagged`. Bit-stealing vs bool-pair.

2. **[Semi-Persistent Vectors](02-semi-persistent-vectors.md)**
   The core primitive. O(1) snapshot, O(k) restore. `InlineStore` vs
   `ParallelStore`. The diff-log protocol. Compile-time elision via
   `const TRACK: bool`.

3. **[`AppendOnlyVec`](03-append-only-vec.md)**
   Push-only vector. Restore = truncate. Useful for interned data,
   string pools, and arena-allocated nodes.

4. **[`Map`](04-map.md)**
   Semi-persistent hash map backed by `AppendOnlyVec` + transient
   `HashMap`. Useful for small registries and symbol tables.

5. **[`ListArena` — Intrusive Linked Lists](05-list-arena.md)**
   Arena of singly-linked list nodes. O(1) prepend, O(n) splice.
   Useful for adjacency lists and dependency tracking.

6. **[`SparseSet` — O(1) Membership](06-sparse-set.md)**
   Three-vector sparse set with O(1) add/remove/contains, stable
   id recycling, and full semi-persistence.

7. **[`BPlusTreeSet` — Arena-Backed B+ Tree](07-bplus-tree.md)**
   Cache-line-aligned B+ tree set with O(log n) insert, O(log n)
   seek, and O(1) step via linked leaves. Semi-persistent via
   a tag bit stolen from the node header.

# Chapter 2 — Semi-Persistent Vectors

[← Ch 1: Dense IDs and Tagged](01-dense-ids-and-tagged.md) · [Table of Contents](00-table-of-contents.md) · [Ch 3: AppendOnlyVec →](03-append-only-vec.md)


## High-Level Idea

Many search algorithms need backtracking: explore a branch, then undo
everything and try a different branch. E-graph equality saturation,
SAT solving, constraint propagation, and game-tree search all share
this pattern. Backtracking requires snapshotting the entire mutable
state and restoring it later.

A naive clone-on-snapshot is O(n) per mark. The semi-persistent
vector takes a different approach: it records negative diffs into a
stack. On `mark()`, the vector stores its current length and starts
logging negative diffs in a frame of its private diff stack. On each
mutation, before overwriting a slot, it pushes the old value into the
negative diff stack. On `restore()`, it replays the log in reverse to
restore captured values, then truncates back to the saved length.
This gives O(1) mark and O(k) restore, where k is the number of
mutations since `mark()`.

The tricky part is the first-write-wins protocol: each slot of the Vec
must be logged at most once per frame (logging the same slot twice wastes
space and the second log entry is useless because only the original value
matters). This requires a per-slot "already captured" flag, which is
exactly the tag bit from Chapter 1. For `DenseId` types stored in
`InlineStore`, the capture flag is the MSB, costing zero extra memory.

### Token Safety

A `mark()` returns an opaque `VecToken`. The caller hands this token
back to `restore()` to undo. But tokens can go stale: if you restore
past a mark and then mark again, you're on a new timeline, and old
tokens from the abandoned future must be rejected. The engine detects
this with two mechanisms:

- `ContainerId` is a globally unique id per `Vec` instance (from an
  atomic counter). Every token records which container it came from.
  `restore()` checks the token's container id matches, preventing
  cross-container bugs (e.g., using one container's token on a different
  container).

- `ForkHistory` is a field in each vector that tracks branch history
  defined by `mark()/restore()` invocations. Each token records a
  `branch_id` and `depth` within that branch. When you restore past
  a mark and re-mark, a new branch is created with a new `branch_id`,
  and the fork point is recorded. On `restore()`, the history is walked
  to verify the token belongs to the current branch or a direct ancestor;
  tokens from terminated sibling branches cause panics.

```rust
pub struct VecToken {
    branch_id: u32,
    depth: u32,
    frame_index: u32,
    container_id: ContainerId,
}
```

## Type Parameters

The vector is parameterized by two type-level concerns and one
compile-time flag:

```rust
pub struct Vec<T, I: IndexLike, S: DiffStore<T, I, TRACK>, const TRACK: bool>
```

`T` is the element type. When stored in an `InlineStore`, `T` must
implement `Tagged` so the capture bit can be packed into each slot's
representation. When stored in a `ParallelStore`, `T` only needs
`Clone` (the capture bits live in a separate `BitSet`).

`I: IndexLike` is the index type. It must admit a bijection to
`[0, N)` for some N determined by its bit width. Concretely,
`IndexLike` requires `to_usize` and `from_usize` conversions, plus
an associated `MAX` constant that bounds the addressable range. The
diff log stores `(I, T)` pairs, so a narrow index type (e.g., `u16`)
keeps diff entries compact.

`DiffStore<T, I, TRACK>` is the storage backend trait, parameterized
by both `T` and `I`. It defines the capture protocol (`capture`,
`prepare_mark`, `restore_entry`) and the raw data access (`get`,
`set_raw`, `push`, `truncate`). `InlineStore` and `ParallelStore`
are the two implementations.

The key unification: `DenseId` implements both `Tagged` and
`IndexLike`. A dense id can be stored in an `InlineStore` (using its
MSB as the capture flag) and simultaneously used as the index type
for a different vector. This is why a single
`VecI<MyId, MyId::Index, TRACK>` can serve as a parent-pointer array:
the elements are id values with zero-cost capture tracking, indexed
by the same id type.

Two convenience aliases capture the common cases:

```rust
type VecI<T: Tagged, I, TRACK> = Vec<T, I, InlineStore<T, I, TRACK>, TRACK>;
type VecP<T: Clone, I, TRACK>  = Vec<T, I, ParallelStore<T, I, TRACK>, TRACK>;
```

## API

| Operation | Cost | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| `push(val)` | O(1) amortized | Append element |
| `pop()` | O(1) | Remove last element |
| `get(i)` | O(1) | Read element at index |
| `view().set(i, val)` | O(1) | Write element (with capture) |
| `len()` | O(1) | Current length |
| `mark()` → `VecToken` | O(1) | Snapshot current state |
| `restore(token)` | O(k) | Undo all mutations since mark |

## The Diff-Log Protocol

### Mark

```
mark():
    push Frame { saved_len: current len, diff_start: diff_log.len() }
    store.prepare_mark(saved_len, &diff_log[prev_frame..])
```

On `InlineStore`, `prepare_mark` clears all tag bits in slots
`[0..saved_len]` so that subsequent mutations can be detected.

### Mutate

```
view().set(i, new_val):
    store.capture(i, saved_len, &mut diff_log)
    store.set_raw(i, new_val)
```

`capture` on `InlineStore`: if `i < saved_len` and tag bit is clear,
log `(old_value, i)` to diff_log and set the tag bit. If tag is
already set, the slot was already captured, so it skips. This ensures each
slot is logged at most once per frame.

`push(val)` just appends to the store; no capture is needed because
truncation on restore handles new elements.

### Restore

```
restore(token):
    frame = frames[token.frame_idx]
    for (old_val, idx) in diff_log[frame.diff_start..].rev():
        store.restore_entry(idx, &old_val, frame.saved_len)
    store.finish_restore(&diff_log[frame.diff_start..], frame.saved_len)
    diff_log.truncate(frame.diff_start)
    store.truncate(frame.saved_len)
    frames.truncate(token.frame_idx)
```

Replays the diff log in reverse order, restoring each modified slot
to the marked value corresponding to the token. Then truncates to the
saved length, removing any diff entries that were pushed after the mark.

## Nested Marks

Marks can be nested. Each `mark()` pushes a new `Frame`. `restore()`
pops back to the specified frame, undoing all intermediate frames.

```
mark()  → token_A (saved_len=10)
  push, push, set(3, x)     // diff_log: [(old_3, 3)]
  mark()  → token_B (saved_len=12)
    set(5, y)                // diff_log: [(old_3, 3), (old_5, 5)]
  restore(token_B)           // undo set(5,y), truncate to 12
restore(token_A)             // undo set(3,x), truncate to 10
```

## `InlineStore` — Zero-Overhead Tracking

```rust
pub struct InlineStore<T: Tagged, I: IndexLike, const TRACK: bool> {
    data: std::vec::Vec<T::Repr>,
}
```

The tag bit lives inside each `T::Repr`. For `DenseId` types, this is
the MSB of the `u32`, costing zero extra memory.

| Operation | Tag behavior |
|-----------|-------------|
| `push(val)` | Store `val.into_repr()` (tag=0) |
| `set_raw(i, val)` | Overwrite repr at `i` |
| `capture(i, ..)` | If `!tag(data[i])`: log old value, `set_tag(data[i])` |
| `prepare_mark(..)` | Clear all tags in `[0..saved_len]` |
| `restore_entry(i, old)` | `data[i] = old.into_repr()` |
| `get(i)` | `T::from_repr(&data[i])` (strips tag) |

## `ParallelStore` — For Non-Taggable Types

```rust
pub struct ParallelStore<T: Clone, I: IndexLike, const TRACK: bool> {
    data: std::vec::Vec<T>,
    bits: BitSet,
}
```

Same protocol, but the capture flag is a separate bit in `BitSet`.
Used for types like `(MyId, u32)` that don't implement `Tagged`.

## `View` — Mutable Accessor

`View` wraps `&mut Vec` and provides `get`/`set` with automatic
capture tracking, preventing accidental calls to `set_raw` without
capture:

## Token Validation

See "Token Safety" above for the full design. In summary:

- `ContainerId` prevents cross-container token use.
- `ForkHistory` prevents stale-branch token use.
- Both checks happen at the start of `restore()` and panic on
  violation; these are always programmer errors, never recoverable.

## `ShrinkPolicy` — Capacity Reclamation

Rust's `Vec::truncate` reduces `len` but preserves `capacity`. After
restoring from a large exploratory branch, the store's internal
arrays may have far more capacity than needed. Capacity ratchets
across branches, converging to the maximum branch size ever explored.

The original design principle: shrinking happens at mark time (before
the frame push), not at restore time. The rationale is that never
shrinking during restore avoids costly reallocations in tight
exploratory loops; the vec naturally "learns" the right capacity.
Shrinking is part of the maintenance phase inside `mark()`, alongside
resize and compaction — semantics-preserving transformations
that are not captured in the diff frames.

```rust
pub enum ShrinkPolicy {
    Never,
    IfOverallocated { factor: usize, headroom: usize },
}
```

With `IfOverallocated { factor, headroom }`, if
`capacity > factor * len`, the backing storage shrinks to
`headroom * len`. The same check is applied to the diff log.

`Never` is best for tight search loops with similar-sized branches.
`IfOverallocated` is for top-level marks after major search resets
where the previous branch was much larger than the next one will be.

All `mark()` methods across the container hierarchy accept a
`ShrinkPolicy`. All `restore()` methods take no policy — they just
undo. Applications typically store the policy as a configurable field
and propagate it through their top-level `mark()` to all
sub-containers.

---
[← Ch 1: Dense IDs and Tagged](01-dense-ids-and-tagged.md) · [Table of Contents](00-table-of-contents.md) · [Ch 3: AppendOnlyVec →](03-append-only-vec.md)

# Semi-Persistent Containers — Design Documents

Semi-persistent data structures with O(1) snapshots and O(k) restoration.
Building blocks for applications that need fast state snapshots and
backtracking — e-graph engines, SAT solvers, constraint propagators,
game-tree searchers, and similar systems.

All containers are built on top of a single core primitive: the
semi-persistent vector (Chapter 2), which uses a diff-log protocol
to record mutations and replay them in reverse on restore.

## Chapters

1. **[Dense Identifiers and the `Tagged` Trait](01-dense-ids-and-tagged.md)**
   How 31-bit ids steal a tag bit for inline capture tracking.
   `DenseId`, `Tagged`, `Opt<T>`, `BoolTagged`. Bit-stealing vs bool-pair.

2. **[Semi-Persistent Vectors](02-semi-persistent-vectors.md)**
   The core primitive. O(1) snapshot, O(k) restore. `InlineStore` vs
   `ParallelStore`. The diff-log protocol. Compile-time elision via
   `const TRACK: bool`.

3. **[`AppendOnlyVec`](03-append-only-vec.md)**
   Push-only vector. Restore = truncate. Useful for interned data,
   string pools, and arena-allocated nodes.

4. **[`Map`](04-map.md)**
   Semi-persistent hash map backed by `AppendOnlyVec` + transient
   `HashMap`. Useful for small registries and symbol tables.

5. **[`ListArena` — Intrusive Linked Lists](05-list-arena.md)**
   Arena of singly-linked list nodes. O(1) prepend, O(n) splice.
   Useful for adjacency lists and dependency tracking.

6. **[`SparseSet` — O(1) Membership](06-sparse-set.md)**
   Three-vector sparse set with O(1) add/remove/contains, stable
   id recycling, and full semi-persistence.

7. **[`BPlusTreeSet` — Arena-Backed B+ Tree](07-bplus-tree.md)**
   Cache-line-aligned B+ tree set with O(log n) insert, O(log n)
   seek, and O(1) step via linked leaves. Semi-persistent via
   a tag bit stolen from the node header.

content.data:
# abstract-domains: Proved Abstract Domain Library

A formally verified implementation of bitvector abstract domains,
written in [Verus](https://github.com/verus-lang/verus) (verified Rust).

## What this is

This crate provides **tristate numbers (Tnums)**, **additive tristate numbers (Anums)**,
**intervals**, **Unums (horizontally composable additive tristate numbers)**, and their
**reduced product TAIU** -- abstract domains for reasoning about bitvector arithmetic
with bitwise uncertainty.

Every operation is verified in two ways:
1. **Formal proofs** (Verus): 754 verified lemmas, 0 admits, proving soundness of the
   abstract domain theory on unbounded natural numbers.
2. **Fuzz tests**: 29 randomized tests, 1M iterations each, checking that the executable
   u64 implementations match the expected concrete semantics.

## Architecture

```
Layer 4: exec_tnum.rs / domains.rs  -- Executable u8/u16/u32/u64/u128 implementations
Layer 3: reg.rs                     -- Bounded register simulation (TnR)
Layer 2: tnum.rs / anum.rs / unum.rs / div.rs -- Unbounded theory with soundness proofs
Layer 1: nats.rs / bools.rs         -- Natural numbers as infinite bitstrings
         tbit.rs                    -- Single-bit abstract domain (Tb)
```

### Layer 1: Infinite bitstrings (bools.rs, nats.rs)

Natural numbers are modeled as infinite boolean strings via `hd` (mod 2) and `tl` (div 2).
All bitwise operations (AND, OR, XOR, AND-NOT) are defined as pointwise `mapd` over bits.
Addition is defined recursively via a full adder with carry.

### Layer 2: Abstract domains (tbit.rs, tnum.rs, anum.rs, unum.rs, div.rs)

**Tnum** `Tn{v, m}`: each bit is independently 0, 1, or unknown (X).
- Membership: `has(n) <==> (n & ~m) == v`
- Precise for: bitwise ops, shifts
- Imprecise for: arithmetic (carry propagation destroys information)

**Anum** `An{v, m}`: a known base value plus bitwise uncertainty.
- Represents: `{v + d | d & ~m == 0}` (v plus any subset of m bits)
- Precise for: addition (base adds exactly, no carry blowup)

**Unum** `Un{v, x}`: a known base value plus a contiguous range.
- Represents: `{v + d | 0 <= d <= x}` (single-field case)
- Multi-field: partitions bits into fields, each a contiguous range [0, max]
- Precise for: addition (fields merge correctly via carry-out detection)
- Key formula: `cout = (x1 & x2) | ((x1 | x2) & ~(x1 + x2))`

**Division**: long division by iterated subtraction, dual of multiplication.
Both constant-divisor and general Tnum/Tnum division are proved sound.

### Layer 3: Bounded simulation (reg.rs)

`TnR{tn, w}` wraps a Tnum with a bit-width. Proves that all operations on
w-bit values produce w-bit results, and that bounded operations simulate
unbounded operations via `chop(_, w)`.

### Layer 4: Executable domains (exec_tnum.rs, domains.rs)

Native Rust implementations on u8/u16/u32/u64/u128:

- **ETn**: Executable Tnum. Well-formedness (`v & m == 0`) proved via `by(bit_vector)`.
- **EAn**: Executable Anum. Exact base arithmetic.
- **EUn**: Executable Unum. Precise addition via carry-out formula.
- **Interval**: `[lo, hi]` bounds tracking.
- **TAI**: Reduced product of Tnum x Anum x Interval x Unum.

The **reduced product** propagates information across domains:
- Interval bounds clear impossible high bits in Tnum and Anum
- Tnum/Anum/Unum min/max tighten the interval
- Unum is rebuilt from tightened interval after bitwise ops
- Unum is threaded directly through arithmetic ops (precise addition)

## Key theorems

| Theorem | File | What it says |
|---------|------|-------------|
| `plus_bv_eq` | tnum.rs | Non-recursive addition formula = recursive |
| `plus_c_carry_decomp` | tbit.rs | The carry compensation property |
| `tn_ext` | tnum.rs | Two inv Tnums with same membership are equal |
| `div_tn_sound` | div.rs | General Tnum/Tnum division is sound |
| `div_const_an_sound` | anum.rs | Anum division by constant with exact base quotient |
| `plus_sound` | unum.rs | Single-field Unum addition is sound |
| `plus_precise` | unum.rs | Single-field Unum addition is precise (no extraneous values) |
| `cout_c_overflow` | unum.rs | Carry-out register = low-bits overflow |
| `plus2_sound` | unum.rs | Two-field Unum addition with boundary preservation |
| `to_an_sound` | unum.rs | Unum to Anum conversion is sound |

## Prerequisites

- [Verus](https://github.com/verus-lang/verus) for formal verification
- [cargo-verus](https://github.com/verus-lang/verus) (`cargo install cargo-verus`)
- Rust toolchain (see `rust-toolchain.toml`)

## Running

```bash
# Verify all proofs (754 lemmas)
cargo verus verify

# Verify only the Unum module (fast, ~5s)
verus --crate-type lib src/lib.rs --verify-module unum

# Run fuzz tests (29 tests, ~12s)
cargo test --test fuzz --release

# Run demo
cargo run --features bin
```

## Verification status

- 754 Verus proofs, 0 admits, 0 errors
- 29 fuzz tests, all passing
- 5 bit-widths: u8, u16, u32, u64, u128
- 4-bit exhaustive: 0 unsound / 1,183,744 Unum addition pairs

## Design documents

- [Unum design](doc/unum-design.md): full explanation of Unums, the carry-out
  formula bug and fix, incomparability with Tnums, TAIU reduced product, and
  proof strategy.
- [Abstract domains design](doc/design.md): overall architecture and proof methodology.

# Abstract Domains Proof Status

**880 verified, 1 error** (exp_128 rlimit), **13 admits** (all in L4 domains.rs)

---

## L1 — Bit primitives (`bools.rs`, `tbit.rs`, `nats.rs`)

All proved. No admits.

### bools.rs — Bit type
- `Bit::full_add` — 3-input full adder, carry + sum

### tbit.rs — TBit (tristate bit)
- `has`, `join`, `meet`, `add_carry`, `add`, `xor`, `or`, `and`, `and_not`
- All soundness lemmas proved

### nats.rs — Infinite bitstring operations (50 proof fns)
- **Bit access**: `hd_tl`, `hd_cons`, `bit_zero`, `bit_tl`, `bit_cons`, `eq_from_bits`
- **Bitwise**: `or_bit`, `xor_bit`, `and_bit`, `and_not_bit`, `mapd_bit`, `mapd_hd_tl`
- **Arithmetic**: `nat_add_carry_correct`, `nat_add_correct`, `nat_add_or`, `disj_bits`, `disj_zero`, `disj_cons`
- **Exponentiation**: `exp_pos`, `exp_8`, `exp_16`, `exp_32`, `exp_64`, `exp_128` ⚠️ rlimit, `exp_concrete`, `exp_eq_pow2`
- **Chopping**: `chop_bit`, `chop_idem`, `chop_disj`, `chop_mapd`, `chop_nat_add`, `chop_nat_mul`, `chop_nat_add_pow2`, `chop_id`, `chop_is_mod`
- **Shift**: `chop_lsh`, `lsh_plus`, `rsh_plus`, `lsh_is_times2`, `lsh_exp`, `rsh_div`
- **Misc**: `xor_ones_complement`, `all_ones_covers`, `all_ones_has`, `all_ones_r_bit`, `bit_exp`, `len_bound`

---

## L2 — Abstract domains over infinite bitstrings

All proved. No admits.

### tnum.rs — Tnum (32 proof fns)
- **Membership**: `has_equiv`
- **Bitwise soundness**: `or_sound`, `and_sound`, `and_not_sound`, `xor_sound`, `bitwise_inv`
- **Shift soundness**: `rsh_sound`, `lsh_sound`, `lshi_sound`, `rshi_sound`
- **Lattice**: `join_sound`, `meet_sound`
- **Add**: `add_carry_sound`, `add_carry_zero`, `add_carry_val_zero`, `add_sound`, `add_bitwise_eq`, `add_inv`, `add_inv_carry`, `add_bitwise_inv`
- **Mul**: `times_b_inv`, `mul_bit_sound`, `chop_sound`
- **Extension**: `tn_ext`

### anum.rs — Anum (19 proof fns)
- **Core**: `from_tnum_sound`, `add_sound`, `add_zero`, `tnum_has_from_anum`
- **Mul**: `mul_step_sound`, `tnum_mul_sound`, `mul_step_r`, `mul_step_anum_sound`, `mul_anum_sound`
- **Div**: `div_const_sound`

### unum.rs — Unum (10 proof fns)
- **Add**: `add_sound`, `add_bounded_sound`
- **Mul**: `mul_sound`, `mul_bounded_sound`
- **Misc**: `offset_bounded`, `offset_from_bound`, `chop_sound`, `to_anum_sound`, `carry_out_c_overflow`

### div.rs — Tnum division/subtraction (12 proof fns)
- `has_bounds`, `join_inv`, `max_has`
- `sub_const_sound`, `lsh_or_tb_sound`, `div_const_sound`
- `neg_tn_sound`, `sub_sound`, `sub_inv`, `div_sound`

---

## L3 — Chopped (bounded-width) domains (`chopped.rs`)

All proved. No admits.

### ChoppedTnum (10 proof fns)
- **Bitwise**: `or_inv`, `and_inv`, `xor_inv`
- **Arithmetic**: `add_sound`, `add_inv`, `mul_sound`
- **Shift**: `rsh_sound`, `lsh_sound`
- **Lattice**: `join_sound`, `meet_sound`

### ChoppedAnum (2 proof fns)
- `add_sound`, `div_const_sound`

### ChoppedUnum (2 proof fns)
- `add_sound`, `mul_sound`

---

## L4 — Executable machine-word domains (`domains.rs`)

Macro-generated for u8, u16, u32, u64, u128.

| `tn_ext` | tnum.rs | Two inv Tnums with same membership are equal |
| `div_tn_sound` | div.rs | General Tnum/Tnum division is sound |
| `div_const_an_sound` | anum.rs | Anum division by constant with exact base quotient |
| `plus_sound` | unum.rs | Single-field Unum addition is sound |
| `plus_precise` | unum.rs | Single-field Unum addition is precise (no extraneous values) |
| `cout_c_overflow` | unum.rs | Carry-out register = low-bits overflow |
| `plus2_sound` | unum.rs | Two-field Unum addition with boundary preservation |
| `to_an_sound` | unum.rs | Unum to Anum conversion is sound |

## Prerequisites

- [Verus](https://github.com/verus-lang/verus) for formal verification
- [cargo-verus](https://github.com/verus-lang/verus) (`cargo install cargo-verus`)
- Rust toolchain (see `rust-toolchain.toml`)

## Running

```bash
# Verify all proofs (754 lemmas)
cargo verus verify

# Verify only the Unum module (fast, ~5s)
verus --crate-type lib src/lib.rs --verify-module unum

# Run fuzz tests (29 tests, ~12s)
cargo test --test fuzz --release

# Run demo
cargo run --features bin
```

- 29 fuzz tests, all passing
- 5 bit-widths: u8, u16, u32, u64, u128

## Design documents

  formula bug and fix, incomparability with Tnums, TAIU reduced product, and
  proof strategy.
- [Abstract domains design](doc/design.md): overall architecture and proof methodology.
